exercises.title: Create your first array
exercises.scenario: Practice the two ways to declare and initialize an array.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to create an int array with values 10, 20, 30 and print the first element.
hints: Array type goes before []
Java arrays are zero-indexed: first element is at index 0

exercises.title: Trace array access
hints: names[0] = Aisha, names[1] = Raj, names[2] = Priya
names.length = 3 (no parens for arrays!)
Output: Raj then 3

exercises.title: Print all elements
exercises.scenario: Use a loop to print every element.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `PrintAll` that:
1. Has an int array with values {5, 10, 15, 20, 25}
2. Uses a for-each loop to print each element

Expected output:
5
10
15
20
25
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your PrintAll class below
// Use for-each loop
hints: For-each syntax: for (int n : nums)
Reads as: 'for each int n in nums'
Inside loop: System.out.println(n)
Cleaner than classic for when you just need values

exercises.title: Sum array elements
exercises.scenario: Use the accumulator pattern with an array.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `SumArray`:
1. Array: {10, 20, 30, 40, 50}
2. Calculate sum using a loop
3. Print 'Total: <sum>'

Expected output:
Total: 150
hints: Initialize total = 0 BEFORE loop
For-each: for (int n : nums)
Inside loop: total += n
Print after loop completes

exercises.title: Indexed print
exercises.scenario: Print with position numbers using classic for loop.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `IndexedPrint`:
1. String array: {"Aisha", "Raj", "Priya"}
2. Print each with 1-based position: '<position>. <name>'

Expected output:
1. Aisha
2. Raj
3. Priya
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your IndexedPrint class below
// Use classic for loop (you need the index)
hints: Classic for: for (int i = 0; i < names.length; i++)
Print 1-based: (i + 1) — wrap in parens for math, not string concat
Without parens: 1Aisha (i+1 becomes string concat)
With parens: '1. Aisha'

exercises.title: Fix the index out of bounds
exercises.instructions:
This code crashes with ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException. Fix it.

Expected output:
10
20
30

bugs.issue: Loop condition `i <= nums.length` makes i reach 3, but valid indexes are 0-2

hints: Array of length 3 has indexes 0, 1, 2
When i = 3, nums[3] doesn't exist
Loop should be `i < nums.length` (strictly less)
Memorize: always `<` for array iteration, never `<=`

exercises.title: Find maximum value
exercises.scenario: Find the largest number in an array — classic interview question.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `FindMax`:
1. Array: {23, 7, 89, 41, 15, 67, 32}
2. Find and print the maximum value

Expected output:
Max: 89
hints: Start max with nums[0] (NOT 0 — safer for any values)
For each element: if n > max, update max
For-each is fine here — we don't need index

exercises.title: Find minimum with index
exercises.scenario: Find both the minimum value AND where it occurs.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `FindMin`:
1. Array: {45, 12, 78, 3, 91, 27}
2. Find min value AND its index
3. Print 'Min: <value> at index <index>'

Expected output:
Min: 3 at index 3
hints: Need both value and index — use classic for loop
Start min = nums[0], minIndex = 0
Start loop at i = 1 (skip the first, which is the initial min)
If nums[i] < min, update BOTH min and minIndex
3 is at index 3 in the array

exercises.title: Calculate average
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Average`:
1. Array: {85.5, 92.0, 78.5, 88.0, 95.5}
2. Calculate average
3. Print 'Average: <value>'

Expected output:
Average: 87.9
hints: Array type: double[] (scores have decimals)
Sum all elements using accumulator
Divide by scores.length (not hardcoded 5)

exercises.title: Search for a value
exercises.scenario: Linear search — does the array contain a target?
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Search`:
1. Array: {17, 42, 89, 13, 56, 91, 24}
2. Search for target = 89
3. Print 'Found at index X' or 'Not found'

Expected output:
Found at index 2
hints: Use a 'sentinel' value: foundIndex = -1 means not found yet
Loop with index: classic for
If match found: set foundIndex = i, then break (stop searching)
After loop: check if foundIndex is still -1
89 is at index 2

exercises.title: Fix the modification bug
exercises.instructions:
This code SHOULD double every element. Instead, it does nothing. Fix it.

Expected output:
10
20
30

bugs.issue: For-each gives a COPY of each element (for primitives). Modifying `n` doesn't change the array.
bugs.fix: Use classic for loop with index: nums[i] = nums[i] * 2

hints: For-each can't MODIFY array elements (it gives copies of primitives)
When modifying, use classic for loop with index
Change first loop to: for (int i = 0; i < nums.length; i++)
Inside: nums[i] = nums[i] * 2 (or nums[i] *= 2)

exercises.title: Count elements matching a condition
exercises.scenario: Count how many elements meet a criterion.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `CountEvens`:
1. Array: {12, 7, 18, 23, 30, 41, 64, 9, 15, 22}
2. Count how many are EVEN
3. Print 'Even count: <count>'

Expected output:
Even count: 5
hints: Counter accumulator: start at 0
For each element: if even (n % 2 == 0), increment count
For-each is fine — just need values
Evens: 12, 18, 30, 64, 22 = 5 numbers

exercises.title: Reverse array print
exercises.scenario: Print array elements in REVERSE order.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `ReversePrint`:
1. Array: {"Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri"}
2. Print elements from LAST to FIRST

Expected output:
Fri
Thu
Wed
Tue
Mon
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your ReversePrint class below
// Iterate from end to beginning
hints: Start at i = days.length - 1 (last valid index)
Loop while i >= 0
Decrement: i--
Java doesn't have negative indexes; must iterate down to 0
Last index = 4 (Fri), then 3 (Thu), ..., 0 (Mon)

exercises.title: Find second largest
exercises.scenario: Find the second-largest element — classic interview problem.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `SecondLargest`:
1. Array: {17, 42, 89, 13, 56, 91, 24}
2. Find the second-largest value
3. Assume all values are distinct

Approach: track largest AND second-largest in one pass.

Expected output:
Largest: 91
Second largest: 89
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your SecondLargest class below
// Track TWO values: largest and secondLargest
hints: Track two values: largest and secondLargest
Start both at Integer.MIN_VALUE (smallest possible int)
For each element n:
- If n > largest: second becomes old largest, largest becomes n
- Else if n > secondLargest: just update secondLargest
This is O(n) — one pass, no sorting needed
Walk through: 91 > 89 > 56 > 42 > 24 > 17 > 13 → Largest 91, second 89

exercises.title: Find pair that sums to target
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `TwoSum`:
1. Array: {3, 8, 11, 6, 15, 2, 7}
2. Target sum: 14
3. Find ANY two indexes i, j (i < j) where nums[i] + nums[j] == target
4. Print 'Pair found at indexes <i> and <j>' or 'No pair found'

Use nested loops (brute force is fine for now — we'll optimize later in DSA).

For target 14: 8 + 6 = 14 (indexes 1 and 3). Or 11 + 3 = 14 (indexes 2 and 0)... wait, 0 < 2. So could be indexes 0 and 2 (3 + 11), OR 1 and 3 (8 + 6), OR 6 and 7 (no, only 7 elements)... Just find the FIRST pair.

Expected output:
Pair found at indexes 0 and 2
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your TwoSum class below
// Use nested loops to find a pair
hints: Outer loop: i from 0 to length-1
Inner loop: j from i+1 to length-1 (j > i ensures we don't pair element with itself, and i < j ordering)
Check if nums[i] + nums[j] == target
When found: print and break out of both loops
Use a `found` boolean to break the outer loop too
First match: i=0, j=2 → 3 + 11 = 14 ✓
